title: Wright's Racing Bulls Future in Doubt as Exit Rumours Gather Pace
meta: Published: 31 March 2026 | By Olly Bradshaw, Paddock Insider | F1CC News
caption: Lukus Wright has scored just one top‑six finish so far in 2026.
body:
Lukus Wright is unhappy at Racing Bulls.

That is the rumour circulating the paddock in the aftermath of the Japanese Grand Prix, and it is not going away.

Sources close to the situation say the 2024 World Champion has become increasingly frustrated with the team's direction, and that an exit — once unthinkable for a driver who won his title in these colours — is now a live possibility.

Racing Bulls declined to comment. Wright's management, when contacted, also declined to comment.

Silence. The loudest kind.

The Numbers That Speak

Wright sits 10th in the drivers' championship with 14 points after four rounds. His teammate, Quarter Kevu, has 28 points — double his tally. A single top‑six finish this season. Strategy calls at the last two races have twice dropped him out of contention. In China, the early safety‑car stop backfired. In Japan, the same gamble left him mired in traffic. The car has pace — Kevu's results prove that — but Wright has not been the beneficiary.

Is the team favouring the reigning champion? No one will say it openly. But the numbers whisper.

The Potential Suitors

Aston Martin, Alpine, and McLaren have all been mentioned in connection with Wright.

McLaren, however, present a problem. Zhao Lei is locked in until the end of 2027. The other seat belongs to a rookie on a long‑term deal. That door appears closed, barring a dramatic change of heart from Woking.

Aston Martin and Alpine are more intriguing.

Aston Martin have Sarah Springfield single‑handedly carrying their constructors' fight, with Sam Fakt yet to score. A Springfield‑Wright pairing would give the team two proven points‑scorers and a championship pedigree. The Honda engine switch has shown promise; a top‑level driver lineup would be the logical next step.

Alpine are a project in flux. Michiii MaraDöner has been a revelation, but Julian Kondra has not scored. The Ford‑powered car has shown flashes of competitiveness, and a driver of Wright's calibre — an LMP2 champion, a Formula 1 world champion — would bring both pace and development expertise. Alpine's long‑term ambitions are no secret.

No Formal Talks — Yet

It must be stressed: no formal negotiations are believed to have taken place. This remains at the level of paddock chatter, of agents sounding out possibilities, of a driver weighing his options.

But the paddock knows that once a driver's unhappiness reaches this level, formal talks are rarely far behind.

Wright's contract situation has not been disclosed. Racing Bulls have a history of stability — they kept Kevu through his title defence and brought Wright back after his Red Bull stint. Yet the dynamic has shifted. Kevu is the team's centre of gravity. Wright, a former champion in his own right, is not a natural number two.

A driver who won 264 points and a world championship in 2024 is not going to accept being a support act indefinitely.

The next race is Bahrain. After that, a sprint weekend in Saudi Arabia, then Miami. Three races in which the driver market chatter will only intensify.

Racing Bulls hold the cards for now. But if Wright wants out, there will be a queue.

Watch this space.
